title: Longing, fragrance, and initial praise
summary_text: The bride opens with longing for her beloved and delight in his fragrance, showing love that is both personal and ecstatic. She is aware of her own complexion and social standing, yet she remains confident in the beauty she has in him. The chapter introduces the poem's movement between private desire and public praise, where the beloved draws her toward his tent and rest.
key_points: The bride longs for kisses and for the nearness of her beloved.
His name and presence are like costly perfume.
She admits her darkness but still calls herself lovely.
The beloved invites her into the safety of his presence.

title: Springtime love and the little foxes
summary_text: The poem turns to spring imagery, with blossoms, birds, and fresh life echoing the joy of mutual love. The beloved calls for attention and warns that small threats can spoil tender growth, which is why the little foxes must be caught. The chapter closes with a plea for steadfast love that lasts until the day is complete and the shadows flee.
key_points: The beloved is pictured as a rose and a lily among thorns.
Love is awakened in the right season, not forced too soon.
Small dangers can damage a growing relationship.
The bride asks to be sealed over the heart as a sign of devotion.

title: Searching at night and the wedding procession
summary_text: The bride searches for her beloved in the night and refuses to rest until she finds him. After finding him and holding him fast, the poem shifts into a public picture of Solomon's royal procession, guarded and honored with splendor. Love is shown as both intimate pursuit and joyful celebration, ending in the security of marriage.
key_points: The bride seeks the one her soul loves until she finds him.
The watchmen cannot give her the love she seeks.
Solomon's carriage is surrounded by warriors and royal splendor.
The chapter ends with love crowned on the wedding day.
evidence_phrases: I sought him whom my soul loves
hold him, and would not let him go

title: The beloved's praise and the locked garden
summary_text: The beloved lavishes detailed praise on the bride, describing her beauty with imagery drawn from flocks, jewelry, mountains, and fountains. He then calls her a garden and a sealed spring, suggesting hidden richness and protected intimacy. The chapter ends with an invitation for the north and south winds to awaken the garden and release its fragrance.
key_points: The beloved sees the bride as beautiful in every part.
She is compared to a garden and a sealed fountain.
Her body and voice are praised with joyful delight.
Love invites the garden to open and release its fragrance.

title: Delayed welcome and renewed longing
summary_text: The beloved arrives, but the bride hesitates, and that delay turns delight into longing. When she finally opens the door, he is gone, and her search becomes painful and public. The chapter closes with her praise of his unmatched beauty, showing that love remains even through loss and discipline.
key_points: Delay can turn sweetness into aching pursuit.
The bride searches through the city and is wounded by the watchmen.
The daughters of Jerusalem ask what makes the beloved unique.
She answers by describing him as altogether lovely.
evidence_phrases: I slept, but my heart was awake
my beloved put in his hand

title: One unique beloved and the bride's beauty
summary_text: As the daughters ask where the beloved has gone, the poem answers by describing him as one in a thousand and unlike any other king or lover. The beloved then turns to praise the bride with the same intensity, calling her beautiful, pure, and uncommon. The chapter presents love as mutual recognition: each sees the other's singular worth.
key_points: The beloved stands out from all others in excellence.
The bride is praised as lovely, pure, and majestic.
She is compared to Tirzah, Jerusalem, and an army with banners.
Love searches for fruit and delight, not competition.

title: Joyful praise and mutual desire
summary_text: The beloved continues to praise the bride from head to foot, using rich, playful images that celebrate her whole person. She is likened to a palm tree, a cluster of grapes, and a lovely garden, while the bride responds with belonging and desire. The poem presents a mature love that is unashamed, generous, and eager for shared delight.
key_points: The beloved delights in every part of the bride.
The bride belongs to her lover and welcomes his nearness.
Their intimacy is compared to fruit, wine, and flowers.
Love becomes stronger as mutual delight deepens.
evidence_phrases: your stature is like a palm tree

title: Love strong as death; seal and vigilance
summary_text: The book ends by comparing love to death, the grave, and an unquenchable fire, insisting that true love cannot be bought or measured. Family concerns, maturity, and responsibility are woven into the final exchanges, but the center remains the bond between the bride and her beloved. The last cry is for haste and reunion, leaving the poem with longing rather than closure.
key_points: Love is powerful enough to rival death itself.
It cannot be purchased or extinguished by wealth.
The bride is pictured as a wall and a fortified peace.
The poem ends with a plea for the beloved to come quickly.
